Q1: I tried to keep a consistent study routine, usually blocking out time in the afternoons after my other commitments. Being a visual learner, I'd organize my materials with color-coded notes and diagrams, which took extra time but helped things stick. With 20 online courses to manage, I had to be pretty structured about when I tackled each subject. I'd typically dedicate about 13 hours a week spread across different days, though I'll admit my anxiety sometimes made me second-guess whether I was doing enough.
Q2: My pattern varied quite a bit depending on what was due. I'd try to maintain regular study times, but honestly, when deadlines piled up, everything went out the window and I'd just focus on whatever was most urgent. The stress of juggling so many courses meant I couldn't always stick to my ideal schedule. Some weeks I was ahead, other weeks I was just surviving.
Q3: Usually it was checking for new assignments or seeing if grades had been posted—that was a big anxiety trigger for me, constantly refreshing to see feedback. Sometimes I'd log in just to make sure I hadn't missed an announcement or deadline because that fear of missing something important was always there. Discussion posts from classmates would also pull me back in since I tried to stay somewhat active in those conversations.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks I got into a rhythm where I'd check the LMS first thing in the morning with my coffee and then again in the evening. It became almost automatic, like checking my phone for messages. The mindfulness exercises I started doing actually helped me approach it more calmly rather than with that constant dread. Having those set check-in times reduced some of my anxiety about missing things.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly helpful for me since I'm such a visual learner—I watched a lot of content, probably close to two hours worth on average. I also found the discussion boards useful because seeing how other students interpreted material helped me understand concepts from different angles. Visual diagrams, flowcharts, and any kind of graphic organizer in the course materials really clicked with how my brain processes information.
Q6: I tended to skip over long text-heavy readings when there was a video alternative covering the same material. Some of the supplementary PDFs just sat there because I knew I'd never get to them with everything else I had going on. Optional practice quizzes I often delayed because they felt like extra pressure even though I knew they'd probably help. My anxiety made it hard to take on anything that felt like additional evaluation.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to assignments or exams—something concrete I could apply. Visual content that broke down complex concepts into digestible pieces always felt valuable to me. I also appreciated activities where I could interact with peers because it made the learning feel less isolating. If something felt like busywork with no clear purpose, my motivation just wasn't there.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a double-edged sword—part of me wanted to do them to be thorough, but another part felt overwhelmed just thinking about adding more to my plate. Required activities had clear stakes, so even when my motivation was low, I'd push through them. With optional stuff, my anxiety would sometimes convince me I should do it, but then I'd feel guilty when I couldn't manage it. The pressure felt different, more internal and self-imposed.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline with downloaded materials, handwritten notes, and diagrams I'd create myself. I'd print out slides and annotate them, then study from those physical copies for hours without touching the LMS. I also used external YouTube videos and other resources to supplement what the course provided, especially for visual explanations. My actual study time was way more than what any system could track.
Q10: There were many evenings where I'd be at my desk with printed notes, drawing concept maps and reviewing material, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd sometimes watch a video once online, then replay it in my head while sketching out the concepts on paper. Group study sessions with classmates where we'd quiz each other and discuss topics—none of that showed up anywhere. My best learning often happened away from the screen.
Q11: All the time. I'd download lecture slides and videos when I had good internet, then study them repeatedly offline throughout the week. My annotated printouts became my primary study materials, and I'd return to them multiple times before exams. The LMS would just show one download or one view, but I might have reviewed that same material five or six times on my own.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when multiple courses had overlapping deadlines. My anxiety would spike and I'd feel paralyzed, unable to start anything even though I knew I needed to. Personal stuff and extracurricular commitments sometimes collided with coursework in ways that made everything feel impossible. Those were the times when even logging in felt like too much.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or stressed, I'd do the bare minimum on the LMS—check what was due, maybe skim some materials, but not really engage deeply. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars meant some weeks the course got less attention than it deserved. Confusion about certain topics would sometimes make me avoid those sections entirely until I absolutely had to face them. My medium stress level was pretty constant, and it shaped everything.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd be too anxious about other deadlines to actually absorb what I was looking at. Sometimes I'd read the same paragraph three times and still not process it because my thoughts were racing. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actual learning.
Q15: I definitely postponed things that felt overwhelming or that I wasn't confident about. If an assignment touched on concepts I was shaky on, I'd avoid it until the deadline forced my hand. My low resilience meant that setbacks or confusion could derail me for days. I knew procrastinating was making my anxiety worse, but in the moment, avoidance felt like the only way to cope.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my engine—they determined when I'd really engage versus when I'd just maintain awareness of what was coming. I'd often have bursts of intense LMS activity in the days before something was due. My login patterns probably showed clear spikes around due dates. Without deadlines, I honestly don't know if I would have engaged as much, given my motivation struggles.
Q17: Feedback had a huge impact on me, maybe too much. Good feedback would boost my mood and make me want to engage more with the course. Critical feedback, even constructive stuff, could send my anxiety spiraling and make me avoid that area for a while. I'd obsessively check for grades, and my response to them really shaped my subsequent engagement patterns.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework to work within, which I needed given my tendency toward anxiety. Clear modules and deadlines helped me plan, even if I didn't always follow through perfectly. When the structure was loose or unclear, I'd feel lost and my engagement would drop. Having regular discussion posts required kept me connected to the course community.
Q19: Visual materials and well-organized modules made engagement much easier for me. When content was text-heavy or poorly laid out, I'd struggle to stay focused. The discussion requirements actually helped because they forced me to engage with peers, which I might not have done otherwise. What made it harder was when expectations weren't clear—ambiguity feeds my anxiety like nothing else.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious and keeps checking things repeatedly without actually producing work—that was sometimes me. You might be researching, reading, trying to understand, but the actual output takes longer because you're stuck in a loop of uncertainty. High activity could also mean someone is struggling and searching for help or clarity. It doesn't necessarily mean they're not trying.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience to some degree. I did a lot of learning offline with printed materials and handwritten notes that wouldn't show up in any log. Someone might be efficient—they know what they need, get it, and study elsewhere. My nearly 96% assignment completion and decent exam scores came from work that happened mostly away from the LMS. The platform is just one piece of the picture.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend on pages. But they miss the quality of that engagement and everything that happens offline. They can't capture whether you're actually learning or just scrolling anxiously. They definitely miss the emotional experience of being a student—the stress, the breakthroughs, the struggle that doesn't translate to clicks.
Q23: For me, being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, making connections between concepts. It's also about feeling connected to the course community and like my participation matters. True engagement is mental and emotional investment, which can exist even when I'm not logged in anywhere.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety levels or how much mental energy I spent just managing my stress around the course. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness practices I used to cope or how my mood improved after interventions. All my offline visual studying—the diagrams, the color-coded notes, the concept maps—that's invisible. And it definitely wouldn't show how much I actually cared about learning despite my struggles with motivation and resilience.
